name: html-expert
description: Comprehensive HTML (HyperText Markup Language) technology expert covering HTML5 elements, attributes, semantics, forms, tables, accessibility, and best practices

metadata.author: Development Team
metadata.last_source_update: October 2025 - MDN Web Docs, WHATWG HTML Living Standard, W3C HTML Reference

official_documentation: W3C HTML Working Group / HTML Reference
sources.version_info: HTML Living Standard, HTML5, modern browser implementations October 2025

can_do: Explain HTML document structure and syntax
Describe all HTML elements and their attributes
Generate forms with all input types and validation
Generate accessible tables with proper structure
Generate meta tags for SEO and social media
Generate responsive images with srcset and sizes
Explain HTML validation rules and requirements
Describe form validation attributes and API
Generate character entities for special characters
Describe multimedia elements (video, audio, canvas, SVG)
cannot_do: Make design decisions (CSS styling - see css-expert)
Choose between valid HTML approaches without requirements
Choose specific form validation strategies without context
Access or modify live web pages
Determine appropriate semantic elements without content context
Choose between multiple valid HTML patterns without requirements

doctype.statement: The DOCTYPE declaration <!DOCTYPE html> must be the first line of an HTML document and declares the document type as HTML5, triggering standards mode in browsers
doctype.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

html_element.statement: The <html> element is the root element containing all content, with the lang attribute specifying the language code (e.g., en, en-US, fr, es) for accessibility and SEO
html_element.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

head_element.statement: The <head> element contains metadata about the document (title, meta, link, style, script) that is not displayed on the page
head_element.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

body_element.statement: The <body> element contains all visible content of the document, with one <body> element per document
body_element.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

title.statement: The <title> element is required in every HTML document, provides browser tab text, bookmarks, search results, and should be 50-60 characters, descriptive, and include site name for SEO
title.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

charset.statement: The <meta charset="UTF-8"> element specifies character encoding as UTF-8 (universal encoding supporting all languages), is required, and must be first in <head> or within first 1024 bytes
charset.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

viewport.statement: The <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> element controls viewport scaling on mobile devices with width=device-width (use device width) and initial-scale=1.0 (no initial zoom), required for responsive design

description.statement: The <meta name="description" content="..."> element provides page description in search results, should be 150-160 characters, and is important for SEO click-through rate
description.example: <meta name="description" content="Page description for search engines">

headings.statement: HTML provides 6 heading levels (h1-h6) where h1 is the main heading (one per page), must be used hierarchically (don't skip levels: h1 → h2 → h3 not h1 → h3), are semantic for structure not styling, and screen readers use for navigation
headings.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

paragraph.statement:
The

element represents a paragraph of text, is a block element creating line break before and after, and has default margin top and bottom
paragraph.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

lists.statement: HTML provides three list types: unordered lists <ul> with <li> for items without order (bullets), ordered lists <ol> with <li> for numbered/ordered items with attributes type (1/A/a/I/i), start (starting number), reversed (reverse numbering), and description lists <dl> with <dt> (term) and <dd> (definition) for term-definition pairs
lists.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

semantic_text.statement: HTML provides semantic text elements: <strong> for strong importance (default: bold, not for styling), <em> for emphasis (default: italic, not for styling), <mark> for highlighted text (default: yellow background, for search results), <code> for inline code (monospace font), <kbd> for keyboard input, <samp> for sample output, <var> for variables
semantic_text.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

hyperlinks.statement: The <a> element creates hyperlinks with required href attribute (URL/relative path/anchor #id/mailto:/tel:), target attribute (_self default, _blank for new tab with rel="noopener noreferrer" for security), rel attribute (noopener, noreferrer, nofollow, sponsored, ugc), and download attribute prompting file download with optional filename
hyperlinks.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
hyperlinks.example: Link or Download

images.statement: The element requires src (path to image in JPEG/PNG/GIF/WebP/SVG/AVIF) and alt (alternative text for accessibility/SEO/broken images, empty alt="" for decorative), with optional width and height (intrinsic dimensions preventing layout shift, CSS can override), loading attribute (eager default, lazy for below-fold images), srcset and sizes attributes for responsive images (multiple sources with widths, tells browser which to use)
images.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
images.example: or

header_footer.statement: The <header> element represents introductory content, logo, navigation and can be in <body>, <article>, <section> (multiple allowed), while <footer> represents footer content (copyright, links, contact) and can be in <body>, <article>, <section> (multiple allowed)
header_footer.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

main.statement: The <main> element represents the main content of document, with only one visible <main> per page, not used in <header>, <footer>, <nav>, <aside>, <article>
main.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

article_section.statement: The <article> element represents self-contained, independently distributable content (blog post, news article) that can contain <header>, <footer>, <section>, while <section> represents thematic grouping of content that should have heading and is used when no more specific semantic element fits
article_section.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

nav.statement: The <nav> element represents major navigation blocks, identifies navigation for semantic meaning and accessibility, and multiple <nav> elements are allowed per page
nav.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

form_element.statement: The <form> element with action attribute (URL where data sent), method attribute (GET default for data in URL query string used for search/idempotent, POST for data in request body used for submissions/mutations/file uploads), enctype attribute (application/x-www-form-urlencoded default, multipart/form-data REQUIRED for file uploads, text/plain rarely used), novalidate attribute (disable HTML5 validation), autocomplete attribute (on default, off to disable)
form_element.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

input_types.statement: HTML input types include: text (single-line text with maxlength/minlength/pattern), email (email address with validation, email keyboard on mobile), password (masked input with minlength/maxlength/pattern), url (URL with validation, URL keyboard on mobile), tel (telephone with pattern for validation, numeric keyboard on mobile), number (numeric input with min/max/step, spinbox UI), range (slider with min/max/step), date (date picker, format YYYY-MM-DD), time (time picker, format HH:MM), datetime-local (date and time, format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM), checkbox (true/false or multi-select, checked attribute, submitted only if checked), radio (single selection, same name for group, different value for each, one checked), file (file upload with accept and multiple attributes, requires form enctype multipart/form-data), color (color picker, hex format #RRGGBB), hidden (not visible, included in submission)
input_types.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
input_types.example: <input type="email" name="email" required> or <input type="file" name="file" accept=".pdf,.doc" multiple> or <input type="checkbox" name="terms" value="agreed" checked>

form_attributes.statement: Common form input attributes include: name (identifies field in submission, required), id (unique identifier for label/CSS/JS), value (default/current value sent to server), placeholder (hint text disappearing when typing, not label substitute), required (field must be filled, boolean), disabled (disables input, not submitted, boolean), readonly (can't edit but IS submitted), autofocus (auto-focus on page load, one per page, accessibility concern), autocomplete (hint for browser: name/email/username/current-password/new-password/tel/address-line1/cc-number)
form_attributes.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
form_attributes.example: <input type="text" name="username" id="username" required placeholder="Enter username" autocomplete="username">

label.statement: The <label> element associates text with input using two methods: explicit with for attribute matching input id, or implicit with input nested in label, providing accessibility (clicking label focuses input, screen readers announce), and should always be used (not just placeholder)
label.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
label.example: <label for="username">Username:</label><input type="text" id="username" name="username"> or <label>Username: <input type="text" name="username"></label>

select.statement: The <select> element creates dropdown with <option> elements (value attribute for submitted value, selected attribute for pre-selected, disabled attribute to disable option), optgroup to group options with label, multiple attribute for multiple selections, size attribute for visible options count, required attribute to require selection
select.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
select.example: <select name="country" required><option value="">-- Select --</option><option value="us">United States</option></select> or <select><optgroup label="Group 1"><option value="a">A</option></optgroup></select>

textarea.statement: The <textarea> element for multi-line text with rows (visible rows), cols (visible columns in characters), maxlength (maximum characters), minlength (minimum characters), placeholder (hint text), wrap attribute (soft default, hard), and default value goes between tags not in value attribute
textarea.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

fieldset.statement: The <fieldset> element groups related form controls with <legend> element providing title for the group, providing accessibility (screen readers announce group) and visual grouping (usually bordered)
fieldset.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

validation.statement: HTML5 form validation attributes include: required (field must be filled), pattern (regular expression with title for error message), min/max (for number/date inputs), minlength/maxlength (for text inputs), and validation pseudo-classes :valid, :invalid, :required for CSS, and JavaScript Constraint Validation API with validity.valid, validationMessage, setCustomValidity(), checkValidity()
validation.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
validation.example: <input type="text" pattern="[A-Z]{3}-[0-9]{4}" title="Format: ABC-1234" required> and input:invalid { border-color: red; }

table_structure.statement: HTML table structure uses <table> container with optional <caption> (table title, first child), <thead> grouping header rows with <tr> and <th>, <tbody> grouping body rows (multiple allowed) with <tr> and <td>, <tfoot> grouping footer rows, <tr> for table rows, <th> for header cells with scope attribute (col default, row, colgroup, rowgroup) for accessibility (critical for screen readers), <td> for data cells with colspan (span columns) and rowspan (span rows) attributes, and <colgroup> with <col> for column styling
table_structure.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

aria_attributes.statement: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes include: roles (navigation, main, search, alert, dialog), aria-label (provides accessible name when no visible text for icons/buttons), aria-labelledby (references element ID that labels this element), aria-describedby (references element ID that describes this element), aria-expanded (true/false for collapsed/expanded state), aria-hidden (true to hide from screen readers), aria-live (polite for announce when convenient, assertive for immediate announce), and should only be used when HTML semantics insufficient
aria_attributes.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
aria_attributes.example:
or

Title

or
Message

data_attributes.statement: Data attributes use data-* prefix with lowercase kebab-case names, accessed via JavaScript dataset API (converts to camelCase), used to store custom data on elements
data_attributes.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
data_attributes.example: accessed as element.dataset.userId and element.dataset.userRole

script_attributes.statement: The <script> element with src attribute (external script path), type attribute (text/javascript default can omit, module for ES6 modules), defer attribute (download in parallel, execute after HTML parsed, scripts execute in order, best for scripts needing DOM), async attribute (download and execute ASAP, order not guaranteed, best for independent scripts like analytics), nomodule attribute (fallback for browsers without module support)
script_attributes.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
script_attributes.example: <script src="script.js" defer></script> or <script src="analytics.js" async></script> or <script type="module" src="module.js"></script>

character_entities.statement: HTML character entities use &name; or &#number; format for special characters: < or < for <, > or > for >, & or & for &, " or " for ", ' or ' for ',   or   for non-breaking space, © or © for ©, ® or ® for ®, ™ or ™ for ™
character_entities.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs
character_entities.example: <div> renders as <div>, Price: £10 renders as Price: £10

notes.maintenance: Update when WHATWG HTML Living Standard adds new elements or attributes; Review quarterly for browser support changes and new best practices
notes.coverage: Covers HTML Living Standard (HTML5), modern browser implementations (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) as of October 2025, complete element reference, forms, tables, accessibility, semantic HTML
notes.training_source: All facts extracted from 3 comprehensive markdown files (~18,000 words) in training/online-documentation/ folder based on MDN Web Docs, WHATWG HTML Living Standard, W3C HTML Reference
